chunk_summary: Defines time-bound criteria for oral and enteral nutrition treatment failure in chronic cardiorespiratory disease to guide timely escalation and prevent prolonged deficits.
learning_objectives: Understand time-bound definitions for oral and enteral nutrition treatment failure in chronic cardiorespiratory disease.
Identify criteria for oral strategy failure, including calorie delivery and weight velocity metrics.
Recognize signs of enteral feeding intolerance and when to escalate to post-pyloric feeding or supplemental PN.
Apply monitoring protocols for inpatient and outpatient rescue phases.
key_facts: Oral strategy failure is defined within 7–14 days if ≥90% of prescribed calories are delivered but no improvement in weight velocity or z-score trajectory is observed.
Tube feeding failure is defined within 7–14 days if calorie targets are unmet due to intolerance (e.g., emesis, aspiration) or unsafe feeding despite first-line modifications.
First intervention for oral feeding failure with <90% calorie delivery is to address delivery issues, not change targets.
Daily weights and intake accounting are recommended during inpatient rescue phases, with weekly weight checks for outpatient care.
Escalation to post-pyloric feeding or supplemental PN should be considered if repeated feed interruptions worsen deficits.
reference_quote: This time-bounded approach aligns with pediatric malnutrition frameworks that emphasize serial measurements and growth velocity, and it creates a clear handover standard across teams.
document_key_topics: Time-bound definitions for oral and enteral treatment failure
Criteria for oral and enteral feeding strategy failure
Monitoring protocols for inpatient and outpatient care
